research_topic: What makes a good landing page hero section
sources_analyzed: nudgenow.com - 10 Best Practices for Hero Sections
blog.logrocket.com - Hero Section Examples and Best Practices
lukcode.com - Psychology Hacks from 500+ Landing Pages

headline.purpose: The moment of clarity - first thing someone reads, decides if they'll stay
best_practices: Speak directly to what the visitor wants, not what you sell
Must be unmistakably clear and outcome-driven
Use natural language, not jargon
Answer: Who are you? What do you offer? Why should I care?
Keep it to one powerful sentence
Use 'framing' technique - wrap title to show what user will gain

good: Build your website faster with tools that actually make sense
Simulate any business decision in seconds
Stop wasting hours build like the top 1%
bad: Understand the story behind your customer clicks and scrolls

subheadline.purpose: The gentle nudge - earns belief after headline earns attention
best_practices: Give context or emotion - the 'how' behind the promise
Explain who the offer is for, what problem it solves, or transformation it creates
Not a repeat of headline - it's a layer beneath it
Should deepen the conversation
subheadline.example: Built for creators who want design freedom without the overwhelm

visual.purpose: The emotional shortcut - translates message into something people can feel
best_practices: Must reinforce the story the copy started
The wrong image dilutes trust as fast as the right one builds it
High-quality and relevant - not stock photos
Product shot, interface demo, or photo mirroring audience reality
Keep it fast, responsive, and intentional
If it doesn't add clarity or emotion, it doesn't belong

types.static_image: Works beautifully when strong, relevant, and well-lit
types.video: Short, muted, optimized for speed and accessibility
types.illustration: Adds personality for brands selling ideas

specs.file_size: Under 200 KB, ideally closer to 100 KB

cta.purpose: The invitation - turns interest into motion
best_practices: Feel like continuation of conversation, not a command
Use active, benefit-focused language matching visitor mindset
Specific and outcome-oriented, not vague
Visually distinct, positioned where eye naturally lands after reading
Add trust notes: 'No credit card required' or 'Start for free'

psychology_principles.five_second_test: Users decide within 5 seconds whether to stay or leave
psychology_principles.cognitive_load_reduction: Keep it simple - one key message, minimal distractions
psychology_principles.visual_hierarchy: Eyes follow: headline → subheadline → visual → CTA
psychology_principles.trust_signals: Customer reviews, security badges, third-party certifications near CTA
psychology_principles.urgency_scarcity: Countdown timers, 'Only X left' - but use sparingly
psychology_principles.emotional_connection: Design appeals to both heart and mind

layout_types.minimalist: Simple but powerful - short heading, one CTA, basic illustration
layout_types.split_column: Left column for heading/welcoming, right for CTA/visual
layout_types.input_capture: Extends CTA with input element (email) to hint at smooth flow
layout_types.video_background: For storytelling brands with strong visuals

requirements: Larger font sizes, high contrast, breathing space
CTAs large enough to tap without zooming
Test by scrolling with one hand - must be effortless

common_mistakes: Unclear messaging - people shouldn't have to decode what you do
Generic visuals - stock photos kill trust instantly
Too much text - you're earning attention, not writing an essay
Weak CTAs - invisible buttons or multiple competing actions
Ignoring mobile - gorgeous desktop hero means nothing if it breaks on phone
Auto-play videos with sound - distracting, drives users away

performance_metrics.bounce_rate: Hero determines if users scroll or exit
performance_metrics.cta_clicks: Track clicks on main CTA as primary success metric
performance_metrics.heatmaps: See where visitors pause, click, or scroll past

trust_elements: Customer ratings and reviews prominently displayed
Payment security icons and privacy badges near CTA
Real photos of people/products vs stock imagery
urgency_techniques: Use sparingly to avoid feeling sales-driven
personalization: Tailor headlines based on visitor behavior/referral source
Match messaging to ad context for seamless transition
Different experiences for new vs returning visitors

industry_specific_notes.saas: Focus on outcome-driven headline, show product interface, clear trial/demo CTA
industry_specific_notes.ecommerce: Show hero product, emphasize value proposition, direct 'Shop Now' CTA
industry_specific_notes.services: Humanize with real team photos, emphasize transformation, consultation CTA
industry_specific_notes.coaching_consulting: Personal photo, credibility markers, book/session CTA

summary: A high-converting hero section has four essential elements working together: a clear outcome-driven headline, contextual subheadline, emotion-evoking visual, and specific action-oriented CTA. It must communicate value within 5 seconds, work flawlessly on mobile, and balance emotional appeal with functional clarity. Simplicity beats complexity - every element must earn its place.
